steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; gallstone pancreatitis (overlap between pancreatitis and biliary etiology); post-partum hepatic pathology (e.g., HELLP-related hepatic hematoma or hepatic congestion)
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is a biliary source (gallstones, choledocholithiasis, biliary ductal dilation, or cholecystitis) driving the markedly elevated lipase/amylase and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and whether the gallbladder/biliary tree shows structural abnormality that would explain or complicate the pancreatitis picture
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones or sludge in the gallbladder, pericholecystic fluid or wall thickening consistent with cholecystitis, and/or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as the precipitant of pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder that would shift the etiology toward hypertriglyceridemia or another cause
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of massively elevated lipase (4555 IU/L) and amylase (2908 IU/L) with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (direct bilirubin 3.4 mg/dL, total 3.7 mg/dL), elevated ALP (243 IU/L), elevated AST/ALT, RUQ-predominant tenderness, and mildly elevated triglycerides makes gallstone/biliary pancreatitis the leading concern, and right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free imaging modality to evaluate the biliary system in this recently post-partum patient.
grounding: Exam: has TTP over epgastric and RUQ, no rebound or guarding
History: acute, constant upper abd, R>L pain around 5pm today after taking some juice
ensemble.reasoning: This post-partum patient has biochemical pancreatitis (lipase >75x upper limit of normal, amylase >29x upper limit of normal) combined with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated ALP, and transaminitis, pointing strongly toward a biliary etiology — most likely gallstone pancreatitis or choledocholithiasis. The RUQ-predominant tenderness and clinical jaundice reinforce this. A right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the logical first imaging step to identify gallstones, assess the common bile duct for dilation, and evaluate the gallbladder wall, as this will determine whether urgent biliary intervention (ERCP) is needed on top of pancreatitis management, while avoiding ionizing radiation in a recently post-partum patient.

History: acute, constant upper abd, R>L pain around 5pm today after taking some juice
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This post-partum patient has biochemical pancreatitis (lipase >75x upper limit of normal, amylase >29x upper limit of normal) combined with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated ALP, and transaminitis, pointing strongly toward a biliary etiology — most likely gallstone pancreatitis or choledocholithiasis. The RUQ-predominant tenderness and clinical jaundice reinforce this. A right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the logical first imaging step to identify gallstones, assess the common bile duct for dilation, and evaluate the gallbladder wall, as this will determine whether urgent biliary intervention (ERCP) is needed on top of pancreatitis management, while avoiding ionizing radiation in a recently post-partum patient.

verification.actual_finding: The gallbladder contains biliary sludge and shadowing stones with trace pericholecystic fluid, and the common bile duct is mildly dilated at 7 mm, consistent with a biliary etiology for pancreatitis.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report directly matched the expected finding of gallstones/sludge in the gallbladder and CBD dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as the precipitant of pancreatitis, along with trace pericholecystic fluid (though without wall thickening).

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction causing gallstone pancreatitis; biliary pancreatitis with common bile duct stone
ensemble.information_gap: Whether a common bile duct stone or biliary obstruction is the underlying etiology of the pancreatitis, given the mildly dilated CBD (7 mm) on ultrasound without a visualized stone, and the markedly elevated bilirubin and liver enzymes alongside massively elevated lipase/amylase
ensemble.expected_finding: A choledocholithiasis (stone in the common bile duct) or biliary stricture/obstruction explaining the dilated CBD, elevated direct bilirubin, and transaminase pattern — or alternatively, characterization of pancreatic parenchymal/ductal pathology (necrosis, ductal disruption, or main pancreatic duct dilation) not visible on ultrasound due to overlying bowel gas
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound demonstrates a mildly dilated CBD (7 mm) without a visualized stone, gallstones with sludge, peripancreatic fluid, and the labs show massively elevated lipase/amylase with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and elevated transaminases, making MRCP the appropriate next step to directly visualize the biliary tree and pancreatic duct for choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology that ultrasound could not resolve.
grounding: Prior imaging: CBD measures 7 mm, which is mildly dilated, although no shadowing stone is identified
Prior imaging: gallbladder is mildly distended, containing biliary sludge and shadowing stones
Prior imaging: small amount of peripancreatic fluid surrounding the head of the pancreas
Prior imaging: portions of the pancreatic tail are obscured by overlying bowel gas
History: acute, constant upper abd, R>L pain around 5pm today
Physical exam: TTP over epigastric and RUQ, mild jaundice
ensemble.reasoning: This postpartum patient has biochemical evidence of severe pancreatitis (lipase 4555, amylase 2908) with a cholestatic pattern (direct bilirubin 3.4, ALP 243, elevated transaminases) and gallstones on ultrasound, strongly suggesting gallstone pancreatitis. The CBD is mildly dilated at 7 mm but no stone was visualized on ultrasound — likely because the distal CBD and pancreatic duct were obscured by bowel gas. MRCP is ordered to directly image the biliary tree and pancreatic duct to confirm or exclude choledocholithiasis and assess for ductal pathology, which would determine whether urgent ERCP is needed.

representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether a common bile duct stone or biliary obstruction is the underlying etiology of the pancreatitis, given the mildly dilated CBD (7 mm) on ultrasound without a visualized stone, and the markedly elevated bilirubin and liver enzymes alongside massively elevated lipase/amylase

representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound demonstrates a mildly dilated CBD (7 mm) without a visualized stone, gallstones with sludge, peripancreatic fluid, and the labs show massively elevated lipase/amylase with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and elevated transaminases, making MRCP the appropriate next step to directly visualize the biliary tree and pancreatic duct for choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology that ultrasound could not resolve.
grounding: Prior imaging: CBD measures 7 mm, which is mildly dilated, although no shadowing stone is identified

Prior imaging: small amount of peripancreatic fluid surrounding the head of the pancreas

History: acute, constant upper abd, R>L pain around 5pm today
Physical exam: TTP over epigastric and RUQ, mild jaundice
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This postpartum patient has biochemical evidence of severe pancreatitis (lipase 4555, amylase 2908) with a cholestatic pattern (direct bilirubin 3.4, ALP 243, elevated transaminases) and gallstones on ultrasound, strongly suggesting gallstone pancreatitis. The CBD is mildly dilated at 7 mm but no stone was visualized on ultrasound — likely because the distal CBD and pancreatic duct were obscured by bowel gas. MRCP is ordered to directly image the biliary tree and pancreatic duct to confirm or exclude choledocholithiasis and assess for ductal pathology, which would determine whether urgent ERCP is needed.

verification.actual_finding: MRCP showed no CBD dilation, no filling defect in the cystic duct or common bile duct, a normal-caliber pancreatic duct, and homogeneous pancreatic enhancement with only mild peripancreatic stranding — effectively ruling out choledocholithiasis, biliary obstruction, pancreatic necrosis, and ductal disruption.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected to find either a CBD stone/stricture explaining the dilated CBD and cholestatic labs, or pancreatic ductal/parenchymal pathology, but MRCP demonstrated none of these findings, leaving the etiology of the markedly elevated bilirubin and pancreatitis unexplained by biliary or structural pancreatic pathology.
